I spend my mornings scrubbing radiation soot off the hydroponic glass, wondering if the Imperial tithe-ships will even bother coming for my harvest this cycle.
My soil is enriched with the powdered remains of a fallen dreadnought, the only way to get enough minerals into the ground to sustain a crop on this wasteland.
I watch the distant flicker of planetary shields failing across the horizon and pray that the encroaching void doesn't swallow my acreage before the winter wheat matures.
The governor's messengers arrived demanding more grain for the front lines, but I told them my fields cannot feed a war that has already lost its way.
I trade my last crates of genetically modified tubers for a handful of rusted capacitors, hoping to keep the atmospheric stabilizers humming for one more season.
I see these executives fighting over the board seats like livestock jostling for the best patch of clover in a crowded pen.
These complex legal contracts are just fences built by city lawyers to keep the neighbors from claiming a piece of the harvest.
I watch the CEO prune the redundant staff with a cold precision that reminds me of cutting back the deadwood to save the orchard.
The way they obsess over quarterly projections is like trying to predict the rain in April without ever looking at the clouds.
This corporate merger is nothing more than two hungry wolves deciding who gets to graze on the smaller sheep first.
I cannot let the river-folk divert the stream, for my first plots of wild grain will wither before the harvest moon.
The chief of the mountain clan claims this valley is theirs, but my hands are stained with the soil I have spent seasons taming.
While the warriors argue over pride and blood, I worry only that the trampling feet of their raiding party will crush my young sprouts.
I offered the elders my finest gourds to broker a peace, hoping a full belly would quiet the urge for war.
If the border stones are moved once more, I will be forced to trade my digging stick for a spear to defend my fields.
I prune the shimmering data-vines that climb the city's central spire, harvesting raw streams of consciousness to feed the hungry districts.
I calibrate the nutrient-mist nozzles in the floating hydroponic plazas, ensuring the neon-wheat glows with a healthy, synthetic radiance.
I listen to the rhythmic humming of the city's geothermal arteries, coaxing the subterranean fungal networks to expand into the abandoned transit tunnels.
I cultivate delicate crystalline orchids in the atmospheric scrubbers, filtering the city's smog into breathable oxygen and iridescent petals.
I shepherd the swarms of pollinator-drones through the vertical orchards, guiding their metallic wings to ensure the city's biological core remains fertile.
I stand before the High Magistrate of Souls, clutching my calloused hands and praying that my harvest of good deeds outweighs the blight of my sins.
The spectral bailiff demanded my testimony, but I can only speak of the soil and the strange, shimmering wheat that grew after the celestial rift opened over my north pasture.
I refuse to let these ghostly prosecutors convince the jury that the blood on my overalls came from anything other than a stubborn calf and a rusty fence.
My defense attorney is a shimmering wraith who claims he can argue my way out of purgatory if I can prove I tended my land with a selfless heart.
As I look upon the shimmering scales of cosmic justice, I wonder if the Great Reaper considers a lifetime of sowing and reaping to be a valid plea for mercy.
I’ve repurposed the cracked hydroponic bays to grow bioluminescent algae, tending to the neon sludge as if it were a field of winter wheat.
The rusted airlocks groan like dying livestock, but I keep them greased and tight to protect my fragile crop from the crushing weight of the abyss.
I spend my mornings scrubbing the salt-crust from the reinforced glass, ensuring every stray photon of artificial light hits the soil.
There is a quiet dignity in harvesting pale, sightless tubers from a pressurized vat while the station creaks under the current.
I treat the leaking coolant pipes like irrigation ditches, carefully diverting the warmth to keep my seedlings from freezing in the deep-sea chill.
I look at this canvas of splattered neon paint and wonder how many bushels of corn it would take to cover a field in such a loud, unnatural color.

This sculpture made of rusted industrial scrap reminds me of the skeletal remains of my grandfather's old tractor, though here it is praised as a statement on urban decay rather than a chore to be cleared from the pasture.
I find it strange that these visitors stand in silence before a blank white wall, for in my world, a void of that size is simply a failure of the soil to yield.
The ambient noise loop of distorted static playing in this gallery sounds exactly like the frantic buzz of a locust swarm descending upon a ripe harvest.

While the curator speaks of the "deconstruction of space," I can only think of how much more honest this room would feel if it smelled of damp earth and honest sweat instead of expensive perfume and sterile air.
I can no longer bring my harvest to the market town, for the gates are barred and the guards shoot anyone who dares approach the walls.
My eldest son’s skin is mottled with those terrible black swellings, and I have no coin left to pay a physician who wouldn't flee in terror anyway.
The fields are choking with weeds and the oxen stand idle, as there are not enough living hands left in the village to guide the plow.
I spent the morning digging a trench in the north pasture, praying that the soil would swallow the dead before the stench drove us all mad.
I watch the crows circle the abandoned cottages of my neighbors, knowing that the silence of the valley is far more frightening than the screams were.
I watched in horror as my prize-winning corn began to flicker between gold and a neon violet, pulsing in time with a sound like static.
The horizon line of my north pasture has developed a jagged tear, revealing a void of scrolling green code where the mountains should be.
I tried to plow the south field today, but my tractor kept snapping back to the barn entrance every few yards as if the world were resetting.
My oldest sheep has started speaking in a layered, metallic chorus, repeating the same three seconds of a greeting over and over again.
I reached for a fence post and my hand passed straight through the wood, feeling nothing but a cold, digital hum vibrating in my fingertips.
